meal_name: Vascular Recovery Sweet Potato & Jasmine Bowl
cooking_steps: Wash and peel the sweet potatoes, then boil them until soft.
Steam the jasmine rice in a separate pot until fluffy.
Wash the Njama-Njama thoroughly. Lightly sauté the leaves in a pan with 1 teaspoon of oil and grated ginger for 5 minutes.
Arrange the boiled sweet potatoes, jasmine rice, and gingered greens together in a large bowl.
Serve warm to instantly restore energy levels after long cardiovascular sessions.

ingredients: 1 cup of warm water or milk
cooking_steps: Boil 1 cup of water or warm milk in a small pot.
Add the oats and stir over low heat for 3 to 4 minutes until soft.
Pour the cooked oats into a wide bowl and let it cool slightly.
Top the oats with diced papaya, sliced banana, and shredded coconut.
Drizzle local honey over the top for natural sweetness.
Eat 30 minutes before a morning run for sustained energy release.
Boil the cassava until soft, peel, and slice into rounds. Set aside to cool.
Rinse the tilapia and pat dry. Season inside and out with salt, pepper, and lemon juice.
Grill the tilapia over medium heat for 6 to 7 minutes on each side until the skin is crispy and the flesh is flaky.
In a bowl, combine chopped tomatoes, sliced onion, cucumber, and a splash of olive oil and lemon juice.
Arrange the cassava slices on a plate, top with the salad, and place the grilled tilapia beside it.
Eat within 2 hours of a long run to replenish protein and minerals.

meal_name: Elite Strength Waterfufu & Eru Warrior Platter
ingredients: 1 piece of roasted local Mackerel fish or lean boiled Goat Meat
A small handful of roasted Groundnuts
cooking_steps: Cook the waterfufu paste in boiling water, stirring vigorously until smooth.
Steam Eru leaves gently with mackerel fish or goat meat.
Mix ingredients to ensure protein juices flavor the greens.
Serve hot waterfufu next to the protein-dense Eru stew.
Eat within 2 hours of a heavy lifting session.

ingredients: 300g of assorted beef and goat meat
Salt and local seasoning to taste
cooking_steps: Bring 4 cups of water to a rolling boil in a deep pot.
Add the ginger, garlic, scotch bonnet peppers, and onion. Boil for 10 minutes to build the spiced broth.
Add the beef and goat meat. Simmer for 20 minutes until the meat is tender.
Add the yam slices and continue cooking for another 15 minutes until soft.
Season with salt and local spices to taste.
Serve hot in a deep bowl. Best consumed after strength training for muscle recovery.
Marinate the beef strips in suya spice mix for 15 minutes.
Heat vegetable oil in a pan and sear the beef strips over high heat for 3 to 4 minutes until browned but still juicy.
Peel and slice the plantains, then pan-fry them until golden on both sides.
Arrange the fried plantains on a plate and top with the suya-spiced beef.
Garnish with sliced onion, tomato, and crushed roasted groundnuts.
Serve warm. Best eaten within 1 hour of a strength training session for maximum protein uptake.

ingredients: 3 clean slices of white or yellow Yam
2 cups of chopped fresh Pumpkin leaves (Ploko)
1 half-teaspoon of unrefined raw Palm Oil
cooking_steps: Boil yam slices until a fork passes through easily. Drain.
Mix ground Egusi with water to form a thick paste.
Boil Egusi paste with pumpkin leaves and a tiny drop of raw palm oil.
Boil lemongrass in hot water separately for 5 minutes for tea.
Serve boiled yam alongside Egusi greens with lemongrass tea.
Wash the lettuce leaves and arrange them as a base on a wide plate.
Layer cucumber and tomato slices over the lettuce.
Add avocado slices on top.
Squeeze fresh lemon juice evenly across the plate.
Sprinkle crushed roasted groundnuts over the top for added protein and crunch.
Drizzle a small amount of olive oil if desired. Best eaten as a light dinner for metabolic reset.
Soak the garri in cold water for 2 minutes, then drain excess water so it stays light and fluffy.
Heat palm oil in a pot and sauté the sliced onion until soft.
Add the chopped okra and stir for 2 minutes.
Pour in the water or stock and add the smoked fish and ground crayfish.
Simmer for 5 minutes until the okra is tender but still firm.
Serve the hot okra soup with the light garri on the side as a small portion.
Best consumed as a dinner to support overnight fat metabolism.
